Tafseer:
Analysis of the word 'Burj':
The word 'Burj' (tower) refers to castles and fortresses. It has been used in this sense in both Arabic and the Qur'an. Where it appears as an attribute of the sky, it refers to the fortresses and observation towers of the sky where Allah Almighty's angels are constantly stationed, ensuring that devils cannot trespass beyond a certain limit into His kingdom—namely, the boundaries of the high assembly. If they dare to cross this limit, then, as you have read in various Surahs, they are pelted with shooting stars. No jinni or human can enter the boundaries of the high assembly or overhear the secrets of the unseen.
The nature of the oath: The oath here, as clarified in various places, is sworn as a testimony or proof for the claim coming next. The addressees of this Surah are those arrogant leaders of Mecca and Ta'if who, in their pride of power, were targeting vulnerable Muslims with persecution. They were so proud of their fortresses and palaces that they never imagined that the All-Knowing and Powerful God—for Whose faith they were persecuting His weak servants—is not a weak entity, but is the Creator and Owner of the sky possessing towers. He is not heedless of His creation; rather, His angels are supervising every corner from the heavenly fortresses and observation towers. Whenever He wills, He can send His dominant hosts from the heavenly towers, or raise some earthly calamity, to demolish all their fortresses, castles, and palaces, and crush the pride of those who boast of them.

The oath of the Resurrection by the Resurrection itself:
This is the oath by the Day of Resurrection which Allah Almighty has promised. Just as He swore by it in Surah Al-Qiyamah, He has sworn by it here. Swearing by the Resurrection to warn of the Resurrection itself points to the reality that it is an obvious truth that no rational person can deny. Thus, in Surah Al-Qiyamah, He stated that its testimony is present within man's own soul, without needing any external proof:
(Rather, man, against himself, will be a witness, even if he presents his excuses) (Al-Qiyamah, 75:14-15).

Pointing to all those testimonies of the Resurrection spread throughout nature:
The indefinite form of 'Shahid' (witness) and 'Mashhood' (witnessed) is for generalization, pointing to all those proofs and testimonies of the Resurrection and recompense spread in every corner of the universe, provided a person possesses eyes and a reflective heart. For example:
- Every small and large thing in this universe bears witness to the Creator's power, wisdom, mercy, providence, and other noble attributes. The obvious requirement of these attributes, as explained in the Qur'an, is that this world should not continue indefinitely without consequence, nor end as an accident, but a Day must come when Allah Almighty distinguishes between the righteous and the wicked. Those who lived in accordance with His pleasure should be rewarded, and those who lived like unbridled beasts should be punished. If this were not to happen, it would mean that to the Creator of the universe, goodness and evil, the righteous and the wicked, are equal.
- The Qur'an has repeatedly narrated the struggle between the messengers and their nations, and its outcome, showing how He devastated those who rejected His messengers, even though they were highly powerful nations. It then invited people to learn a lesson from their ruins, reminding them: We preserved these ruins in the earth so that people might see them and take warning, knowing that what Allah did to them, He will do to you as well if you adopt their way and display their pride.
Alongside this argumentative aspect, the words 'Shahid' and 'Mashhood' also contain an element of warning regarding what people will face on the Day of Resurrection—namely: no one should remain under the delusion that what he does in the world is hidden from God; rather, whatever he does will appear before him one by one, and he will see everything with his own eyes. His own limbs will testify to his every word and action. The guardians appointed by Allah (kiraman katibeen) will present their record of his good and bad deeds, and the prophets, righteous, and reformers will also testify to what they taught and how people treated them and their teachings.

Warning to the deniers of the Resurrection:
This is not the response (jawab) to the oaths, but as in Surah Qaf and some other Surahs, the response is omitted here, and in its place, the verses of warning and reminder for the deniers of the Resurrection are presented. This method is adopted where the response is so clear that the mind is easily directed to it without explicit mention. This creates conciseness, and the entire subsequently described event can be regarded as the response to the oaths, as dictated by the context. Opening the omitted response in the light of the oaths, it is: the Resurrection is certain, nothing is outside the grasp of Allah's power, and on that Day, everyone will face the consequence of what they did.
'Ukhdud' means a trench, ditch, or pit. This is explained by: 'The Fire full of fuel' (an-nari dhati-l-waqood). That is: these trenches will be filled with Fire having fuel. Describing it as having fuel is to show that Allah Almighty has arranged abundant fuel to keep this Fire burning constantly, so no one should expect it to ever grow dim. The nature of this fuel is described in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 24, as:
(fear the Fire, whose fuel is men and stones).
'As-hab-ul-Ukhdud' (the companions of the trench): Under this, commentators have narrated the story of a king, without specifying his name or era. They merely state that he threw many believing Christians of his time into trenches of Fire because they refused to prostrate to him. Although some incidents of burning one another are recorded in history during the theological conflicts among the People of the Book, the specific details narrated by commentators regarding this event are in no way reliable; therefore, we ignore them. The primary addressees here, as noted, are those leaders of the Quraysh who were persecuting weak Muslims. What lesson could they learn from the end of an unknown king whose name was unknown to them and to the commentators alike? Furthermore, it should be noted that the warning and rebuke for 'As-hab-ul-Ukhdud' relate to the Hereafter, not to this world. If we interpret it as relating to this world, it merely portrays their tyranny, but the Qur'an has not mentioned any exemplary end they faced in this world that could serve as a lesson for the Quraysh. The statement that their own Fire turned back and burnt them is mentioned only by commentators; the Qur'an has not pointed to it, although that was the primary point to be displayed for the objective.

In our view, this is a warning of Hell to those leaders of the Quraysh who were persecuting the Muslims to turn them away from faith. They are warned: if they do not abandon this cruelty, they will be cast into the trench of Hell, which is filled with a never-extinguishing Fire.

Depicting the end of the wicked:
This portrays the end of the wicked. It is stated: they should remember the time when they will sit by that trench, witnessing what they did to the Muslims with their own eyes. The meaning is: first, they will be made to sit by the trench of Hell to see their resting place, and then they will taste the consequence of their every cruel action. Thus, 'shuhood' (witnesses) is used in the sense of the effect of their action, which is common in the Qur'an.
Note that if a criminal is shown his place of punishment beforehand, and then made to suffer it, his punishment is doubled. The Qur'an repeatedly indicates that the criminals will first be made to stand at the edges of Hell to see where they are to go, and then commanded to enter. Regarding Pharaoh and his people, it is stated that they are shown the Fire morning and evening in the barzakh.
It may be asked that 'idh' (when) is used for a past event, whereas we have interpreted it as a future event. However, this doubt is resolved by the fact that the conditions of the Resurrection are repeatedly described in past tenses in the Qur'an, which is a style showing the certainty of its future occurrence.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 6 and 7 was combined in the original text.]

The greatest virtue of the believers is the greatest crime in the eyes of the disbelievers:
That is: this wrath against the Muslims is not because they have committed any offense, but their greatest virtue is regarded by these wicked individuals as their greatest crime, for which they are being punished. They are being punished for the 'sin' of believing in Allah, the All-Mighty, the Praiseworthy. Yet, by believing in Him, these servants completed the greatest duty placed on them by their Creator. Their action deserved acknowledgment, honor, and emulation, not hostility and revenge; but those who lose their intellect treat their well-wishers in this manner.
The requirements of the attributes 'Al-Aziz' (the All-Mighty) and 'Al-Hameed' (the Praiseworthy): Here, reference is made to two attributes of Allah. 'Al-Aziz' manifests His honor, power, status, and majesty, and 'Al-Hameed' manifests His mercy, providence, and His being worthy of praise and gratitude. Referencing them is intended to show: the Being Who possesses these attributes is the One Who is worthy of faith. Those who believe in Him have taken His support, which is the true support, and they alone will succeed. This contains comfort for the oppressed Muslims and a threat for their persecutors.

This further clarifies the preceding comfort and threat: the One Who possesses the kingdom of the heavens and earth is the One Who is worthy of faith, and His protection is sufficient for those who believe in Him.
'And Allah, over all things, is Witness' (wa-llahu 'ala kulli shay'in shaheed) - That is: those Muslims who are suffering at the hands of the enemies should remain assured that the Owner of the kingdom of the heavens and earth is not unaware of their difficulties, but is witnessing everything. Since He is witnessing everything, how long will He tolerate this tyranny against His believing servants in His kingdom! At the same time, this is a warning to the disbelievers: do not grow arrogant due to His respite. Allah is not unaware of your cruelties; He is witnessing everything, and the time is near when He will take retribution for His oppressed servants.

Explicit warning to those who persecute Muslims:
What was stated earlier in the form of indications is made entirely explicit in this verse. It is stated: those who persecute believing men and believing women to turn them away from faith and do not repent will face the punishment of Hell, specifically the punishment of the Burning Fire.
The word 'fitnah' here refers specifically to the persecution inflicted upon someone to turn them away from their faith. This word is used in this sense in various places of the Qur'an.
The mention of 'believing women' (mu'minat) alongside 'believing men' (mu'mineen) is made with special care because during that period of trial, the greatest cruelties were inflicted on the weak, particularly slave-girls. Those who inflict these cruelties are warned: if they desire safety, they should repent and reform immediately; otherwise, if they die in this state, they will enter Hell.
After 'the punishment of Hell' ('adhabu jahannam), there was apparently no need to mention 'the punishment of the Burning Fire' ('adhabu-l-hareeq), but this is a specific mention after a general mention. Hell is the center of all punishments, and the greatest of them is burning. Thus, they are warned that along with the other punishments of Hell, they will also have to taste the punishment of burning. Let them reflect on this end.

Glad tiding to those who stand firm:
This encourages those believers who stood firm on their faith despite these difficult trials. The verb 'amanu' (believed) indicates that it carries its true and perfect meaning here. The meaning is: those who stand firm on their faith despite the cruelties of the wrongdoers, and continue in their righteous deeds, will have gardens beneath which rivers flow.
'That is the great attainment' (dhalika-l-fawzu-l-kabeer) - That is: no one should regard this success as minor. This is an eternal and everlasting success, and only those who achieve it will know what a magnificent kingdom they received in return for a few days of trials.

Confirming the preceding points through His attributes:
Confirming the threats to the disbelievers and comfort to the oppressed, Allah Almighty has reinforced them through His attributes.
'Indeed, the vengeance of your Lord is severe' (inna batsha rabbika lashadeed) - It is stated: the seizure of your Lord is extremely severe. No one should remain under the delusion that he can save himself through his power, population, or intercessors. No one can escape His seizure, nor can anyone save another from Him.
Addressing the Prophet as 'your Lord' (rabbika), the address is to him as the representative of these oppressed.

'Indeed, He it is Who originates and repeats' (innahu huwa yubdi'u wa yu'eed) - This is the proof for the threat of Hell stated above: no one should remain under the delusion that the Hereafter and recompense are merely threats, and there is no life after death. It is stated: He alone is the One Who originates creation in this world, and since He originates it, He can easily repeat and recreate it. Since He faced no difficulty in the first creation, why should the second be difficult for Him!

'And He is the Forgiving, the Loving, Owner of the Throne, the Glorious, Doer of what He intends' (wa huwa-l-ghafooru-l-wadood, dhu-l-'arshi-l-majeed, fa'alul-lima yureed) - That is: He alone is the One Who forgives and loves, so let whoever wishes to connect themselves do so with Him alone. No one else can benefit another. He alone is the Owner of the Throne, and possesses all majesty. No one is His partner in His sovereignty, and He does whatever He intends. He is not dependent on anyone, nor can anyone hinder His decrees.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 14, 15, and 16 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 14, 15, and 16 was combined in the original text.]

Referencing some historical examples:
To prove the realities stated above, reference is made to some historical examples. Among the nations who faced His seizure, the Qur'an has mentioned the power, tyranny, and rebelliousness of Thamud and Pharaoh with special care. The leaders of the Quraysh were highly awed by the power of these two nations. Pointing to them, it is asked: see what end they faced when He seized them—they were erased from the face of the earth, and no one could save them!
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 17 and 18 was combined in the original text.]

The actual cause of denial is stubbornness:
Before 'bal' (but), a portion of the discourse is omitted based on context. If unpacked, the full sentence is: 'What is communicated to them is indeed the absolute truth, and there is no room to deny it, but those who disbelieve persist in denial, and they are now determined not to believe.'

The word 'wara'' means both before and behind. The meaning is: if they are determined to persist in denial, let them do so, but their denial will not turn the truth into falsehood. Allah encompasses them from all sides, and they cannot escape Him.

Before this 'bal' as well, a portion is omitted based on context: 'This Qur'an that warns you of your end is indeed a solid reality. It is a glorious klam (speech). It is not a baseless statement of poets or soothsayers, but is revealed from Allah, originating from the Preserved Tablet, which is protected from the reach of any jinn or human.'
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 21 and 22 was combined in the original text.]
